summary: Gruber traces the extraordinary saga of Jeff Bezos's phone being hacked via a malicious WhatsApp attachment sent personally by Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman. The hack exfiltrated data from Bezos's phone, and intimate photos revealing an extramarital affair ended up published in The National Enquirer — though a separate WSJ report suggests the photos may have come from his girlfriend's brother. Gruber connects the threads between Bezos, MBS, the Khashoggi murder, Trump, Kushner's own WhatsApp communications with MBS, and the Enquirer's long history of protecting Trump. He frames the entire interconnected scandal as too outlandish for a movie pitch, proposing it as a season-long TV series. In footnotes, he digresses into Bezos's writing ability and speculates that MBS likely didn't hack Kushner because the political cost would outweigh the intelligence value.
key_points: UN forensic analysis found it 'highly probable' that MBS personally sent Bezos a malicious WhatsApp video that compromised his phone, leading to massive data exfiltration
The leaked intimate photos may have actually come from Bezos's girlfriend's brother — a Trump supporter with ties to Roger Stone — rather than directly from the Saudi hack
The Enquirer's David Pecker attempted to extort Bezos into dropping his investigation, which Bezos publicly exposed in a remarkable open letter
Gruber draws a damning web connecting MBS, Trump, Kushner's WhatsApp use, the Khashoggi murder, and the Enquirer's decades of protecting Trump
Trump's antipathy toward Bezos effectively gave the Saudis a free pass to hack him — the UN is more outraged than the US president
money_quotes: Needless to say, Bezos told Pecker to fuck off, in a remarkably cogent open letter publicly revealing both the extortion scheme and Bezos's investigative team's suspicion that the Saudis were the culprits.
Trump's strident antipathy toward Bezos effectively served as a free pass for the Saudis to hack his phone.
That the United Nations is more outraged than the United States says it all.
This whole saga is extraordinary to say the least. With zero hyperbole, it sounds like the pitch for a Hollywood thriller.
I take it back, this is not the pitch for a movie. It's the pitch for a season-long TV series.
Writing is thinking, I've always thought, too. I frequently start a column thinking my argument is A, but as I write, I realize I was wrong and in fact my argument is Z.
Hacking the phone of someone in his family actually would.
key_insight: The Bezos phone hack reveals a web of geopolitical corruption so interconnected — spanning Saudi intelligence, tabloid extortion, presidential conflicts of interest, and a journalist's murder — that the US president's personal vendettas effectively gave a foreign regime license to attack an American citizen.
